methodology_sections: 2.3 Data Flow Tracing - Input flow, external data, asset movement, permission escalation
3.2 Business Logic Analysis - Economic incentives, edge cases, assumption violations, race conditions

data_flows.source: Seaport order fulfillment (zoneParams calldata from Seaport)
path: Seaport calls Create.validateOrder()
Decode RentPayload and signature from zoneParams.extraData
Construct SeaportPayload from zoneParams
Validate protocol signature expiration, fulfiller, and signer role
_rentFromZone processes the payload
_isValidOrderMetadata verifies metadata hash against zoneHash
_isValidSafeOwner verifies fulfiller owns the safe
_executionInvariantChecks verifies assets went to correct destinations
_convertToItems creates Item[] from offers and considerations
For BASE/PAY: accumulate rentalAssetUpdates, build RentalOrder, derive orderHash
STORE.addRentals stores order hash and rental asset IDs
ESCRW.increaseDeposit records ERC20 deposits
_addHooks calls external hooks
transformations: SpentItem[] -> Item[] (with itemType and settleTo assignment)
Item[] -> RentalId[] (via toRentalId with recipient wallet)
data_flows.trust_changes: Untrusted Seaport calldata transitions to trusted after protocol signer signature verification and invariant checks; hook calls re-enter untrusted external code
validation_points: _isValidOrderMetadata checks rentDuration != 0 and metadata hash == zoneHash
_convertToItems enforces item type rules per orderType
exposure_risks: RentPayload metadata.orderType is NOT included in the metadata hash, so fulfiller can alter it
RentPayload metadata.emittedExtraData is NOT included in the metadata hash, so fulfiller can inject arbitrary event data
Hook.onStart() receives rental data to potentially malicious contract
manipulation_risks: Fulfiller can change orderType without invalidating zoneHash (orderType missing from _deriveOrderMetadataHash)
rentalWallet is missing from _deriveRentalOrderHash, enabling potential hash collisions across different wallets
Hook contracts receive extraData and can perform arbitrary logic

data_flows.source: User calls Stop.stopRent(order) with full RentalOrder struct
path: Validate rental can be stopped based on orderType, expiry, and sender
Build rentalAssetUpdates from order items
_removeHooks calls external hooks (onStop)
_reclaimRentedItems: Safe.execTransactionFromModule -> delegatecall Reclaimer.reclaimRentalOrder
Reclaimer transfers ERC721/ERC1155 from safe to lender
ESCRW.settlePayment calculates and distributes ERC20 payments
STORE.removeRentals deletes order hash and decrements rental asset counts
transformations: Item[] -> RentalAssetUpdate[] (rental items with rental IDs)
Payment amount -> paymentAmount - fee (if fee active)
Pro-rata: amount split by elapsed/total time ratio
sinks: ERC721/ERC1155 transferred from rental wallet to lender
ERC20 transferred from escrow to lender and/or renter
data_flows.trust_changes: User-supplied RentalOrder is trusted only after its hash matches storage; external calls to hooks and NFT receivers introduce untrusted execution before state updates
validation_points: Reclaimer checks address(this) != original and address(this) == rentalWallet
exposure_risks: rentalWallet not in orderHash allows supplying different wallet with matching hash
External calls (hooks, NFT transfers) occur before storage removal (CEI violation)
Lender receives callback during NFT transfer before order is removed from storage
manipulation_risks: Potential reentrancy through ERC721/ERC1155 receiver callbacks on lender address before STORE.removeRentals
Order with manipulated rentalWallet could pass hash check if rentalWallet omission is exploited
stopRentBatch processes all reclaims before all payments, amplifying reentrancy window

data_flows.source: Safe transaction initiated by rental wallet owner
path: Safe calls Guard.checkTransaction before execution
Guard loads function selector from calldata
Guard checks if hook exists for target address
If hook active: forward to hook.onTransaction (bypasses _checkTransaction)
If no hook: _checkTransaction inspects selector against blocklist
For NFT transfer selectors: load tokenId from calldata and check isRentedOut
For module enable/disable: check whitelist
Guard.checkAfterExecution is called but does nothing
transformations: Raw calldata -> tokenId at known offset (assembly mload)
Raw calldata -> extension address at known offset
sinks: Transaction allowed to proceed or reverted
data_flows.trust_changes: Safe owner's transaction is untrusted; validated by guard logic or delegated to hook
validation_points: Selector-based checks for known dangerous functions
exposure_risks: When hook is active, _checkTransaction is completely bypassed
Unknown selectors (not in the if/else chain) are allowed by default
ERC20 token transfers are not blocked (design choice but exposure risk)
manipulation_risks: A malicious/buggy hook could approve dangerous operations that _checkTransaction would block
Non-standard NFT contracts with different transfer function signatures bypass guard checks
The guard doesn't prevent setting approvals on non-rented NFTs, which could set up future exploits

path: Calculate elapsedTime and totalTime from rental timestamps
Loop through items, filter ERC20 payments
Calculate fee if active, deduct from paymentAmount
Decrease deposit (balanceOf[token] -= item.amount pre-fee)
If PAY order and not expired: pro-rata split between lender and renter
If PAY order and expired, or BASE order: full payment to settleTo recipient
data_flows.trust_changes: Order data trusted by permissioned modifier; ERC20 transfer target addresses from order struct
validation_points: permissioned modifier ensures caller is authorized policy
exposure_risks: Fee remains in escrow as trapped balance, only recoverable via skim()
If ERC20 is fee-on-transfer, actual received amount < recorded amount, creating accounting mismatch
manipulation_risks: Fee-on-transfer tokens would cause balanceOf to be inflated relative to actual token balance
Rebasing tokens could cause escrow balance to diverge from tracked balanceOf
Pro-rata rounding always favors lender (renter gets bumped up by 500, but lender gets remainder)

path: Executor selects action and target
Based on action type: install/upgrade module, activate/deactivate policy, migrate kernel, change executor/admin
Module install: verify contract, validate keycode, store mappings, call INIT
Module upgrade: swap addresses, call INIT, reconfigure dependent policies
Policy activate: grant permissions, add to active list, configure dependencies
Policy deactivate: revoke permissions, remove from active list, prune dependents
data_flows.trust_changes: Only executor can perform actions; no timelock or multi-sig requirement at kernel level
exposure_risks: Single executor address has full control over protocol configuration
ChangeExecutor and ChangeAdmin do not require target to be a contract
manipulation_risks: Executor can install malicious modules or policies
Module upgrade reconfigures policies which calls external configureDependencies()
Kernel migration disables all policies without re-enabling them on new kernel

core_rules: Only protocol-deployed rental safes can be used for renting
BASE orders: lender offers NFTs, renter pays ERC20; renter gets nothing back on expiry unless PAY
PAY orders: lender offers NFTs + ERC20 payment; renter receives pro-rata payment if stopped early
PAYEE orders: mirror of PAY orders, processed without storage footprint
Rental assets cannot be transferred from safe while rental is active (guard enforced)
Only the Stop policy module can reclaim assets from rental safes
Delegate calls from rental safes restricted to whitelisted addresses
Protocol signer must authorize all rental creation payloads
economic_incentives: Protocol takes a fee (0-100%) on all rental payments during settlement
Fees accumulate in escrow as delta between tracked and actual balance, withdrawn via skim()
PAY order pro-rata split incentivizes renters to use full rental duration
Lenders in PAY orders can stop early, receiving unused payment back
BASE order payments always go fully to lender upon expiry
governance_mechanisms: Kernel executor controls module installation, upgrades, policy activation/deactivation
Kernel admin controls role granting and revoking
ADMIN_ADMIN role manages whitelists, fees, and proxy upgrades
GUARD_ADMIN role manages hook paths and statuses
CREATE_SIGNER role authorizes rental creation payloads
No timelock on any governance action

edge_cases.scenario: orderType field is NOT included in _deriveOrderMetadataHash despite being in the EIP-712 type string
edge_cases.expected_behavior: orderType should be committed in the zoneHash so the lender's signed order type cannot be changed
edge_cases.actual_behavior: The fulfiller can supply any orderType value in OrderMetadata without invalidating the zoneHash check, because only rentDuration and hooks are hashed
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: While item type enforcement in _convertToItems limits cross-type manipulation, the missing commitment violates the security model. If new order types are added or item validation changes, this becomes directly exploitable. Currently emittedExtraData is also uncommitted, allowing injection of arbitrary event data.

edge_cases.scenario: rentalWallet is missing from _deriveRentalOrderHash encoding despite being in the EIP-712 type string
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Each rental order hash should uniquely identify the order including which wallet holds the rented assets
edge_cases.actual_behavior: Two orders identical in all fields except rentalWallet produce the same hash. The type hash includes 'address rentalWallet' but the abi.encode call skips it, going directly from order.renter to order.startTimestamp
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: During stopRent, an attacker can supply a RentalOrder with a different rentalWallet that matches the stored hash. This could cause assets to be reclaimed from the wrong wallet and payments settled to wrong parties. While Storage.removeRentals would likely revert due to rentalId mismatch, ERC1155 tokens rented to multiple wallets could create matching rentalId counts enabling exploitation.

edge_cases.scenario: Check-Effects-Interactions violation in stopRent: external calls before storage removal
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Storage should be updated before external calls to prevent reentrancy
edge_cases.actual_behavior: stopRent calls _removeHooks (external), _reclaimRentedItems (external NFT transfers with callbacks), ESCRW.settlePayment (external ERC20 transfers) all before STORE.removeRentals
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: A malicious lender contract receiving ERC721/ERC1155 via onERC721Received/onERC1155Received callback could re-enter stopRent while the order is still in storage. While same-order reentrancy would likely fail (NFTs already transferred) or cause the original tx to revert, edge cases with ERC1155 amounts > 1 or cross-order interactions in stopRentBatch could be problematic.

edge_cases.scenario: Hook active for target address bypasses all Guard._checkTransaction protections
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Guard should enforce base security checks even when hooks are active
edge_cases.actual_behavior: When a hook is registered and active for a target, _checkTransaction is NOT called. The hook's onTransaction is solely responsible for security. If the hook doesn't replicate guard checks, setGuard/enableModule/disableModule/transfer operations could be permitted.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: A GUARD_ADMIN could set a hook for an address that doesn't enforce module/guard protection checks, allowing the safe owner to remove the guard or disable the stop policy module, then freely transfer rented assets.

edge_cases.scenario: Fee-on-transfer ERC20 tokens used as rental payment
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Escrow tracked balance should match actual received amount
edge_cases.actual_behavior: ESCRW.increaseDeposit records the nominal amount from the Seaport order, but if the token charges a transfer fee, the actual received amount is less. This inflates balanceOf relative to true balance, and settlement will fail when insufficient actual tokens exist.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: An attacker can create a rental with a fee-on-transfer token. The escrow tracks full amount but holds less. During settlement, if multiple orders use the same token, later settlements fail due to insufficient balance. The skim function would also underflow (trueBalance < syncedBalance).

edge_cases.scenario: EIP-712 _deriveHookHash does not keccak256 the bytes extraData field
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Per EIP-712 spec, dynamic bytes should be hashed with keccak256 before encoding
edge_cases.actual_behavior: _deriveHookHash passes hook.extraData directly to abi.encode, which encodes it as a dynamic type with offset/length/data instead of its keccak256 hash
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Internal consistency means this doesn't create an on-chain exploit, but off-chain tools using standard EIP-712 encoding would produce different hashes, potentially causing integration issues.

edge_cases.scenario: rentalOrderTypeHash computed with abi.encode instead of abi.encodePacked for nested type strings
edge_cases.expected_behavior: EIP-712 type hash = keccak256(concatenation of type strings)
edge_cases.actual_behavior: _deriveRentalTypehashes uses abi.encode(rentalOrderTypeString, hookTypeString, itemTypeString) which adds ABI encoding padding, while rentPayloadTypeHash correctly uses abi.encodePacked
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Internal consistency prevents on-chain exploit, but the inconsistency between rentalOrderTypeHash (abi.encode) and rentPayloadTypeHash (abi.encodePacked) indicates a code quality issue. Off-chain verification tools would need custom handling.

edge_cases.scenario: Domain separator is immutable, does not handle chain forks
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Domain separator should be recomputed if chain ID changes (fork scenario)
edge_cases.actual_behavior: _DOMAIN_SEPARATOR is computed once at deployment and stored as immutable. _CHAIN_ID is stored but never checked against block.chainid at runtime. After a chain fork, signatures are replayable on both chains.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: After a chain fork, rental creation signatures can be replayed on the forked chain, potentially creating unwanted rentals. The protocol signer's authorization is valid on both chains.

edge_cases.scenario: stopRentBatch settles all payments after all reclaims, widening reentrancy window
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Each order's payments should be settled atomically with its reclaim
edge_cases.actual_behavior: All _reclaimRentedItems calls happen in a loop, then ESCRW.settlePaymentBatch is called for all orders at once, then STORE.removeRentalsBatch removes all. A callback during any reclaim occurs before ANY payment is settled and before ANY order is removed from storage.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: During reclaim of order[0], a callback could call stopRent for order[1] (not yet processed in batch), fully settling and removing it. The batch would then fail when processing order[1] reclaim. While the batch reverts, it creates a griefing vector.

edge_cases.scenario: Guard allows pure ETH transfers to be blocked
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Rental safes should be able to receive and send ETH
edge_cases.actual_behavior: Guard.checkTransaction reverts if data.length < 4, blocking pure ETH transfers (empty calldata). Rental safe owners cannot send ETH without at least 4 bytes of calldata.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Not directly exploitable but limits legitimate safe functionality. ETH sent to the safe may become trapped if there's no way to send it out.

edge_cases.scenario: Create2Deployer marks address as deployed before successful deployment
edge_cases.expected_behavior: deployed mapping should only be set after successful creation
edge_cases.actual_behavior: deployed[targetDeploymentAddress] = true is set before the assembly create2 call. If create2 fails, the subsequent address check reverts, but the deployed mapping is already set in the transaction's state changes. Since the entire tx reverts, this is actually safe in normal cases.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: Not exploitable because the revert undoes the mapping change. But if the check were removed or bypassed, it would permanently prevent redeployment.

edge_cases.scenario: PAYEE orders have no storage footprint and cannot be independently stopped
edge_cases.expected_behavior: All rental orders should be trackable and stoppable
edge_cases.actual_behavior: PAYEE orders skip the if block in _rentFromZone that adds rentals to storage, increases deposits, and processes hooks. They have no order hash in storage and no rental asset tracking.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: PAYEE orders cannot be stopped via stopRent since there's no order hash in storage. If the PAY counterpart is stopped but the PAYEE items were already transferred, there's no mechanism to reclaim PAYEE-side assets independently.

edge_cases.scenario: Pro-rata calculation overflow with large amounts and timestamps
edge_cases.expected_behavior: Calculation should handle large values safely
edge_cases.actual_behavior: (amount * elapsedTime) * 1000 could overflow for large amounts and durations. Solidity 0.8.x checked arithmetic would revert, making settlement impossible.
edge_cases.exploitation_potential: An attacker could create a rental with a very large ERC20 amount and long duration such that the pro-rata calculation overflows, preventing early settlement of PAY orders. The lender would need to wait for full expiry to settle.

assumption_violations: Assumes all ERC20 tokens transfer the exact amount specified (violated by fee-on-transfer tokens)
Assumes all ERC20 tokens have standard return values (partially handled by _safeTransfer)
Assumes ERC721/ERC1155 safeTransferFrom callbacks don't perform malicious reentrancy
Assumes hooks approved by GUARD_ADMIN are not malicious or buggy
Assumes the protocol signer's private key is not compromised
Assumes Seaport correctly enforces order matching and prevents double-fulfillment
Assumes rental wallet owners cannot bypass the guard through unknown function selectors
Assumes the chain ID never changes (no fork handling for domain separator)
Assumes lender and renter addresses are not contracts that could exploit callbacks
game_theory_risks: Lender can stop PAY orders early to limit renter's payment, especially if renter usage increases asset value
Renter in BASE order has no incentive to return assets early since they paid full upfront
Protocol signer is a centralized point of trust; compromise allows unauthorized rental creation
Hooks add middleware that could censor or manipulate rental lifecycle events
An attacker could create many small rentals to grief the guard's isRentedOut checks with many storage reads
integration_risks: Seaport order types and zone callback interface could change in future versions
Gnosis Safe module/guard interface changes could break Stop/Guard policies
ERC20 tokens with non-standard behavior (rebasing, pausable, blacklistable) can break escrow accounting
Hook contracts are external dependencies that can revert and block rental start/stop
If Seaport processes multiple orders atomically and one fails, all fail, which could be used to grief
calculation_errors: OrderMetadata hash computation omits orderType and emittedExtraData fields despite them being in the type string, creating a 3-slot encoding where a 5-slot encoding is specified
RentalOrder hash computation omits rentalWallet field despite it being in the type string, creating an 8-field encoding where 9 fields are specified
rentalOrderTypeHash uses abi.encode instead of abi.encodePacked for concatenating type strings, producing a different hash than EIP-712 specification
Hook hash uses raw bytes encoding of extraData instead of keccak256(extraData) as required by EIP-712 for dynamic bytes types

token_semantics.notes: Protocol uses custom _safeTransfer handling bool/void returns. Fee-on-transfer tokens will cause balanceOf accounting mismatch in PaymentEscrow. Rebasing tokens will cause skim() underflow. No SafeERC20 wrapper used.
Transferred to rental safe during creation and reclaimed to lender on stop. safeTransferFrom triggers onERC721Received callback on recipient, which is a reentrancy vector when lender is a contract.

Amount tracked in rentedAssets mapping. safeTransferFrom triggers onERC1155Received callback. Batch transfers completely blocked by guard. Multiple concurrent rentals of same token+id across wallets possible, interacting with rentalWallet hash omission.

invariants.name: order_hash_uniqueness
invariants.notes: VIOLATED: rentalWallet is omitted from _deriveRentalOrderHash, so orders differing only in rentalWallet share the same hash. orders[hash] = true can only represent one order.

invariants.name: escrow_balance_conservation
invariants.notes: balanceOf[token] tracks deposits and is decremented on settlement. Fees accumulate as delta between tracked and actual balance. Fee-on-transfer tokens break this: actual < tracked. skim() would underflow.

invariants.name: rental_asset_tracking
invariants.notes: Incremented on rental creation, decremented on stop. RentalId = keccak256(wallet, token, tokenId). If rentalWallet is altered during stopRent via hash collision, wrong rentalId would be decremented.

invariants.name: guard_transfer_prevention
invariants.notes: Guard blocks known transfer selectors for rented assets. Bypassed when hook is active for target. Non-standard transfer functions and ERC20 transfers are not blocked.

invariants.name: metadata_hash_completeness
invariants.notes: VIOLATED: orderType and emittedExtraData are NOT included in _deriveOrderMetadataHash despite being in the EIP-712 type string. Fulfiller can alter these without invalidating zoneHash.
